title: Spec Builders Roundtable: Financing, Margins, and Lessons from the Field
summary: A peer roundtable of spec builders from across the US shared their experiences transitioning from flips to new construction, with deep discussion on financing structures, investor relationships, and profit margin targets. Participants covered strategies for sourcing private and institutional capital, the tradeoffs of pre-selling versus selling finished homes, and how to position spec builds in competitive markets. The group also explored price point strategy, production versus custom approaches, and the challenges of being a first-time spec builder.

teachings.concept: Equity Partnership Model for Spec Builds
teachings.explanation: A common financing structure where a developer contributes zero cash out of pocket, a capital partner funds the land purchase (often 20% of project cost), a bank funds 80% of construction, and profits are split 50/50. The developer earns a GC fee (~10% of build cost) and a real estate commission on top of their profit share, compensating them for labor and expertise.
teachings.quote: I use partners on all my new builds. So I come in with no money out of pocket. Partner gets 50% of the profit. I get 50% of profit. I do charge a GC fee of 10% ish of the build price. And I do get a real estate commission on the backend.

teachings.concept: 25% Annualized Return as the Investor Threshold
teachings.explanation: When seeking private investors for spec projects, offering below a 25% annualized return makes it difficult to attract capital because less risky investment alternatives exist. The 25% floor accounts for the illiquidity and risk premium investors require for real estate development.
teachings.quote: 25 is the number. Below that it's tough to find money. Below 20, like, cause there's less risky investments.

teachings.concept: 20% Return on Total Project Cost as Developer Minimum
teachings.explanation: Targeting a 20% return on total project costs (including interest, selling fees, and holding costs) provides a safety buffer so that if market conditions deteriorate, the developer is not in a losing position. This metric differs from return on equity and accounts for all capital deployed.
teachings.quote: I want to make a 20% return on costs. So the total project costs after interest and selling fees and holding costs, I want to make 20% on the money that I have invested.

teachings.explanation: Listing a spec home only when it is 100% complete, fully staged, and pre-inspected maximizes price and minimizes days on market. Buyers make emotional decisions and struggle to visualize unfinished spaces, so presenting a finished product gives one clean shot at the market at peak perceived value.
teachings.quote: You get one shot to hit the market. You want to hit the market at the right time of the year for you, for your specific market, but finished punch list on staged. Pre-inspected, like ready to go.

teachings.explanation: Listing a spec home during construction inflates days-on-market figures, which signals problems to buyers and depresses perceived value. Agents can strategically time listings, use off-market or private exclusive periods, and relist after the MLS-required reset window to avoid this perception problem.
teachings.quote: You could have a projected end date for your listing while it's under construction. And then you take it off the market for whatever your MLS requires you to, to reset the clock.

teachings.concept: Pre-Selling Tradeoff: Funding Certainty vs. Management Complexity
teachings.explanation: Pre-selling a spec home guarantees the project is funded before completion but dramatically increases management complexity, as every decision must be approved by buyers and their extended circle. Builders should price in this additional time and effort or offset it with a large non-refundable deposit and strictly limited customization options.
teachings.quote: It took 4 million percent more effort on my part to get it to the finish line than a spec house. Every decision has to run by the husband, the wife, the kid, the aunt, the uncle, their friend is an architect.

teachings.concept: Price Point Strategy: Avoiding the Middle
teachings.explanation: Spec builders should target either the upper price tier (typically $1.5M+) or stay below the production builder threshold. The mid-range faces the worst competition—price-sensitive buyers who are rate-sensitive, and direct competition from production builders who can deliver faster and cheaper at scale.
teachings.quote: I need to be in the 1.5 million plus because down at the 600,000 I'm competing with all of these other companies that build a track home and just slap them up super quick and I don't want to be competing with that anyway.

teachings.concept: Investor Psychology: Living Vicariously Through Builders
teachings.explanation: Many private investors are drawn to real estate development not purely for financial return but because they are emotionally engaged with the building process and enjoy telling others about their involvement. Understanding this motivation helps builders frame investment pitches as opportunities rather than asks.
teachings.quote: A lot of the people that invest in me are kind of like they wish they were builders or developers and they're kind of living vicariously through me.

teachings.concept: Keeping Investor Count Low Per Project
teachings.explanation: Limiting each project to a single investor simplifies communication, reduces the time spent managing relationships, and avoids conflicts between multiple stakeholders. Having fewer investors with more capital each is preferable to syndicating small amounts from many people.
teachings.quote: I only have one investor per project cause I don't want to be babysitting a bunch of people. So that's like, I think about how many investors you want to have. And I try to keep that number as low as possible.

actionable_advice: Build relationships with at least three local banks for construction lending, since lenders periodically stop offering spec construction loans and you need backup options.
Use owned land as collateral for a bank construction loan before considering giving up 50% equity to a private investor, as the cost of capital is dramatically lower.
Do not list a spec home on MLS during construction; wait until it is 100% complete, staged, and pre-inspected to maximize price and minimize days on market.
If you must pre-sell during construction, take a large non-refundable deposit (e.g., $200K or ~20%) and limit buyer customization strictly to cosmetic choices like fixtures, not structural changes.
When pitching private investors, prepare a clean presentation deck to signal organization and professionalism—this alone builds credibility even if the strategy is not novel.
Tell everyone in your network what you are doing and make it easy for potential investors to say no; you want people who are genuinely enthusiastic, not reluctant participants.
Approach mortgage brokers as potential investors, as they frequently seek real estate LP opportunities.
If investors fund the entire project (land plus construction), model the deal in Excel with exact cash-in and cash-out dates to calculate true annualized returns accurately.
For lower price point specs, lock all finishes and selections before sales and do not allow customization, as the margin is too thin to absorb change order management costs.
Use a designer with a fixed budget for finishes and lighting rather than making all selections yourself, to save time and ensure cohesive design at controlled cost.
client_challenges: How do you find and attract private investors when you are a first-time spec builder with no track record?
Spec home has been on the market for 2.5 months with only lowball offers—unclear whether the issue is pricing, timing, or market conditions.
Tiffany is constrained to one local bank willing to lend on spec homes, leaving her dependent on a single lender's terms and appetite.
Creed is navigating a land-seller partnership offer that would require hard money financing, uncertain whether the risk-return tradeoff is worth it.
Martha owns lots outright and wants to build her first spec home but is unsure how to structure financing without giving up too much equity.
How do you structure and document private money loans from friends and family in a legally sound way?
Listing the spec home during construction inflated days-on-market figures, making buyers question what is wrong with the property.
Creed questions whether his $1.15M spec home was built at the wrong price point for the current market, wondering if going higher-end would reduce buyer interest-rate sensitivity.
notable_quotes: On pre-selling complexity: 'It took 4 million percent more effort on my part to get it to the finish line than a spec house. Every decision has to run by the husband, the wife, the kid, the aunt, the uncle, their friend is an architect.'
On flipping ethics vs. competitiveness: 'The only way to make money in flips in Houston is to shut your eyes when you see a problem and just paper over it. And that's not something that I can do.'

On investor pitching mindset: 'We can get in our heads about asking for money but it's really just giving people an opportunity to support something that they think is cool and make some money while they're doing it.'

On building a brand through spec homes: 'It'll give you a lot of credibility in the construction world and the real estate world. My average price point on the sales side just skyrocketing. Start doing spec houses, it'll open up a lot of doors for you.'
On investor return threshold: '25 is the number. Below that it's tough to find money. Below 20, cause there's less risky investments.'

On presentation materials: 'Having something like this just—it's a good presentation and I've gotten a lot of good feedback that like, oh you look organized. It's like, oh this is all it takes for you to believe in me? Okay cool.'
